title: Flu Vaccine Safety and Pregnancy | Influenza (Flu) | CDC
text:

Previously, it was recommended that people with severe allergy to egg (those who have had any symptom other than hives with egg exposure) be vaccinated in an inpatient or outpatient medical setting. Beginning with the 2023-2024 season, additional safety measures are no longer recommended for flu vaccination of people with an egg allergy beyond those recommended for receipt of any vaccine, regardless of the severity of previous reaction to egg. All vaccines should be given in settings where allergic reactions can be recognized and treated quickly. 
Keep Reading: 
Vaccines and People with Egg Allergies 
Monitoring safety of flu vaccines during pregnancy 
CDC and FDA conduct ongoing safety monitoring of vaccines licensed for use in the United States. 
CDC and FDA monitor flu vaccine safety during pregnancy during each flu season using the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) : an early warning system that helps CDC and FDA monitor for health concerns (also termed "adverse events") following vaccination. Anyone can report adverse events after vaccination to VAERS. Generally, information from VAERS reports cannot determine if an adverse event after vaccination was caused by a vaccine, but t
